does: This paper introduces BELLS-O, an independent operational benchmark for evaluating LLM supervision systems including input/output moderation filters and jailbreak detectors. The benchmark comprehensively assesses 28 different supervision systems from 17 providers—covering both specialized guardrails like LlamaGuard-4 and ShieldGemma-2 as well as frontier generalist LLMs repurposed as supervisors such as GPT-5.4 and Claude Sonnet—across multiple performance dimensions. The evaluation jointly measures detection rates, false-positive rates, latency, and monetary cost across 11 harm categories for input/output moderation and 13 attack techniques for jailbreak detection, using carefully constructed datasets combining handcrafted prompts, expert-curated samples, and quality-controlled synthetic data designed to suppress generator fingerprints.
connects: This work addresses a critical gap in existing supervision system evaluation practices that suffer from vendor bias, incomplete cost accounting, and limited comparative analysis between specialized guardrails and generalist models. It builds on prior work in AI safety benchmarking and red-teaming methodologies while extending them to operational concerns—latency and cost—that have been largely absent from academic oversight evaluations. The paper responds to the growing deployment of LLM-based supervision systems as primary safeguards in production systems and recognizes the need for independent evaluation similar to how other security systems are assessed, moving beyond proprietary vendor benchmarks that naturally favor their own solutions.
enables: By providing systematic operational trade-off data across diverse supervision systems, this benchmark enables informed decisions about which systems to deploy in production given specific latency and budget constraints. It establishes a reusable evaluation framework and dataset infrastructure that future supervision system developers can benchmark against, creating accountability and driving improvements in detection performance without sacrificing operational efficiency. The findings likely reveal that different supervision approaches have distinct trade-offs—some specialized guardrails may excel at detection while others minimize cost or latency—suggesting that optimal safeguarding may require hybrid approaches or that operational considerations should influence supervision system design choices moving forward.
